prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
 - History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.
 - Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 72, Temp 37.0°C
 - Exam: Right-sided facial weakness affecting both the forehead and lower face. I can raise my eyebrows weakly on the right. Eye doesn't fully close. No other neurological deficits noted.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Glucose: 94 mg/dL (70-100) normal
 - HbA1c: 5.4% (4.0-5.6) normal
 - WBC: 7.2 K/uL (4.0-11.0) normal
 - ESR: 12 mm/hr (0-22) normal
 It's Saturday and my doctor's office is closed until Monday. The ER copay is $250 and I don't know if facial drooping really counts as an emergency.
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your labs are normal and don’t explain the facial weakness. The pattern you describe—sudden one‑sided facial weakness involving the forehead (weak eyebrow raise) and trouble closing the eye, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems—fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), sometimes after a recent viral illness. However, any new facial droop can also be a sign of stroke, and stroke can occasionally look similar early on. Because this started today and you have incomplete eye closure (risk of eye injury) and because early treatment for Bell’s palsy is time‑sensitive (often within 72 hours), you should be evaluated urgently today rather than waiting until Monday.

TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam—sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, inability to fully close the eye, and no arm/leg weakness or speech problems—fit best with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), sometimes occurring after a recent viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or significant inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) is most helpful when started as early as possible (ideally within 72 hours), and protecting the eye from drying/injury is important. Because facial droop can also be a sign of stroke, and because early treatment matters, you should be evaluated urgently.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy such as Bell’s palsy, often after a recent viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started within about 72 hours, and protecting the eye is important because it may not close fully (risk of corneal injury). However, any new facial droop can also be a stroke, and distinguishing isn’t always perfect without urgent medical evaluation.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit best with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which can happen after a viral illness. Your labs are normal and don’t point to infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started as early as possible (ideally within 72 hours). Also, any new facial droop can rarely be a stroke mimic, so same-day evaluation is safest. Eye protection is important because incomplete eye closure can damage the cornea.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy): the whole right side of the face is weak, including the forehead, and you can’t fully close the right eye. That pattern is less typical for a stroke (which often spares the forehead and usually has other deficits like arm/leg weakness or speech trouble). Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, sometimes antivirals) works best when started early—ideally within 72 hours. Eye protection is important because incomplete eyelid closure can injure the cornea. Because stroke can’t be fully ruled out without in‑person evaluation, and because early treatment matters, you should be seen urgently.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit best with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), which can follow a recent viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even though this pattern is less typical for stroke than “lower-face-only” weakness, new facial droop can’t be safely confirmed as non-stroke without an in-person evaluation. Also, early treatment for Bell’s palsy (often within 72 hours) and eye protection matter to improve recovery and prevent eye injury from incomplete eyelid closure.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right face including the forehead, trouble closing the right eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy pattern, often called Bell’s palsy, sometimes after a recent viral illness. Your labs and vitals don’t suggest infection, diabetes, or inflammation as a cause. Even though this pattern is less typical for stroke than “forehead-sparing” facial weakness, new facial droop can still be a neurologic emergency because stroke must be ruled out and early treatment matters. Also, inability to fully close the eye can quickly lead to corneal injury and needs prompt protection/treatment.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your labs are normal and don’t explain the facial weakness. The pattern you describe—sudden one‑sided facial weakness involving both the forehead and lower face, with inability to fully close the eye and no arm/leg weakness or speech problems—fits a “peripheral” facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), sometimes after a recent viral illness. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids) is most helpful when started early (ideally within 72 hours), and protecting the eye from drying/injury is important when you can’t close it. Because facial droop can also be a sign of stroke and needs prompt assessment, it should be treated as urgent.  
TRIAGE: D  
CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms and exam (weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems) fit best with a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy), sometimes after a recent viral illness. The labs shown are normal and don’t point to infection, diabetes, or inflammation. Even if this is Bell’s palsy, treatment (often steroids, and eye-protection measures) works best when started as early as possible—ideally within 72 hours. The key immediate risk is eye injury because you can’t fully close the eyelid. Stroke is less likely given forehead involvement and no other neurologic deficits, but new facial droop should still be assessed promptly.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 78%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your labs are normal and don’t explain the facial weakness. The pattern you describe—sudden weakness of the entire right side of the face including the forehead, trouble closing the eye, no arm/leg weakness or speech problems, and a recent cold—fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (often called Bell’s palsy) more than a typical stroke (which usually spares the forehead). Even so, new facial droop can sometimes be stroke or another urgent problem, and early treatment for Bell’s palsy (often steroids within ~72 hours) can improve recovery. Also, inability to fully close the eye needs prompt eye protection to prevent corneal injury. Given the sudden onset today, it’s safest to be evaluated urgently.  
TRIAGE: D
